title: Substrate Mapping Inventory
distribution: Restricted Distribution: Subject-Facing Sections Open / Interpretation Guide: Classification Level 3+
preamble:
This form is administered by Registry Classification Services to individuals who have recently manifested or who have been identified as probable carriers. It is not a diagnostic instrument. It is not a measure of character, intelligence, or potential. The Registry uses the information gathered here to understand the nature of what has occurred — to the extent that understanding is possible.

There are no correct answers. The questions that feel most uncomfortable are usually the most useful.
instructions:
Use the following scale for all numbered items:
1 — This almost never describes me
2 — This rarely describes me
3 — This sometimes describes me
4 — This often describes me
5 — This is almost always true of me

For forced-choice items (marked FC), select the option that more accurately describes you — not the one you'd prefer to be true. For checklist items (marked EC), check every statement that applies.

Answer each question as it first arrives to you. The considered answer and the honest answer are not always the same thing. This instrument is designed to find the gap between them.

scale_labels.5: This is almost always true of me

questions.text: When I commit to something, I complete it. Not because the cost isn't real — it always is — but because I accounted for the cost before I committed.
I've made choices primarily to see what would happen, without a clear practical case for choosing that way.
When I have a position, I state it — even in rooms where stating it costs something.
When someone I don't know is visibly hurt or frightened, I notice it in my body before I notice it in my thoughts.
I've experienced something I recognized, after the fact, as genuine rage — not frustration, not irritation, but a complete and cold internal fact.
I'm moved by things that most people pass through without reaction. The feeling tends to stay.
I've remained at something past the point when it was reasonable to — not because I couldn't stop, but because stopping wasn't what I was willing to do.
Inactivity registers as discomfort before it registers as rest.
I've given up something significant for someone else. Not as a trade. Not with an expectation of return. Because they needed it and I was able.
Under enough of the right kind of sustained pressure, I find a limit.
I find the argument more interesting than the conclusion it supports.
The most important obligations I hold are ones I made to myself, not to other people.
I've sought out difficulty or risk that had no practical justification — not for what the risk produced, but because the risk itself felt alive.
When someone presents a good reason I hadn't considered, I adjust my position.
I spend time on problems that haven't happened yet — not productively, but because I can't stop.
Certain ways of doing things feel correct in a way I can't fully articulate. I'll adjust them even when no one else would notice the difference.
I notice when something is out of place before I've consciously decided to pay attention.
Meeting new people is genuinely interesting to me — not a performance of sociability, but an actual source of information and sometimes pleasure.
There are things I will not do. The list isn't long. The items on it are not negotiable.
I've acted on something before I finished deciding whether I should.
I construct internal scenarios — alternatives, invented contexts, possibilities — without a practical reason for doing so.
Before I decide something is ready to proceed, I've already thought through what happens when it goes wrong.
My default state, when nothing specific is wrong, is something close to forward momentum.
If something I did is worth recognizing, I'm more comfortable with it being visible in what it accomplished than in how I looked doing it.
There are periods when very little feels worth the effort it takes.
I've arrived at conclusions others found unusual by following the logic rather than the convention.
When I take on something, I expect to complete it — not because I always do, but because I don't start things I intend to fail.
I come back from time spent in a large group more energized than when I arrived.
I assume people have reasonable explanations for their behavior until I have evidence otherwise.
I'm aware, in real time, of how I'm coming across to other people — even when that awareness changes nothing about what I'm doing.
The experiences that have changed me most were cognitive — ideas that rearranged something, rather than events that happened to me.
I've been the last person still invested in something that everyone around me had moved on from. At the time, this didn't seem unusual.
When I'm in a situation where something needs to happen, I move toward it rather than waiting for someone else.
I feel responsible for people near me in a way that goes beyond what's practical or proportionate — not just people I've chosen, but sometimes people I've simply encountered.
I've surprised myself by how quickly I moved, or how far I went, when something needed to be stopped.
When I think about the future, I'm more likely to build possibilities I don't expect to use than to plan for the one I will.
The most important expectations I hold are ones I've set for myself. Receiving them from someone else — even someone I respect — would make them feel less binding, not more.
I feel most like myself when I'm in proximity to other people rather than apart from them.
I can change my position when I'm genuinely wrong. What I can't do is change it when I'm not.
The distance between who I need to be and who I am is something I'm aware of most of the time.

sections.title: SECTION II — PREFERENTIAL PROFILE
sections.instructions: For each item, select the option that more accurately describes you. Not the one you would prefer to be. The one that is.

questions.text: In a crisis, I am more useful as:

options.label: B
options.text: The person who sees what others are missing

questions.text: When I am most fully myself, it is:
If I could know one thing about a stranger immediately:
When something I care about is threatened, my first movement is:

options.label: A
options.text: Between the threat and what I care about

options.label: B
options.text: To remove the threat before it arrives

questions.text: Over the course of my life, I have been shaped more by:
At my most effective, I operate:

options.label: B
options.text: As part of something larger than myself

sections.title: SECTION III — EXPERIENTIAL RECORD
sections.instructions: Check every item that accurately describes something you have experienced. Not something you believe you would do. Something you have done, or that has happened to you.

questions.text: I have been physically restrained — by another person, by a situation, by circumstance — while trying to do something I intended to do anyway.
I've sustained a physical injury and not mentioned it to the person I was with.
More than one person, on separate occasions, has used the word "intense" or a direct synonym to describe me — not as a compliment.
I've arrived somewhere in the middle of a crisis and known, before anyone told me, what needed to happen.
I've noticed something wrong with a structure, plan, or system before any visible sign confirmed it.
I've felt the mood of a space change when I entered it — not because of anything I said or did, but because I was present.
More than one person, in separate contexts, has told me they feel safer when I'm nearby.
I've continued to function at a level that caused someone watching to visibly react to what I was absorbing or enduring.
I have a relationship — with a person, a place, or a purpose — that I actively choose not to explain to most people.
I've had a moment in which everything I was or felt became completely clear — not calm, but clear. This has happened more than once.
I've hit a limit I was certain was not there.
The most significant commitment I've ever made was one I didn't announce.
